title: The Two Ledgers Every Indigenous-Owned Enterprise and Social Enterprise Runs, and Why Only One Is Actually Profit
linkedin:
NAIDOC Week is a good moment to name something we see across Indigenous-owned enterprises and social enterprises all year round: two ledgers sitting inside one set of accounts, and only one of them is actually profit.

Grant funding, sponsorship and government contracts land in the same P&L as trading revenue from the cafe, the consultancy or the retail line. Blended together, the combined result can show a comfortable surplus for the year.

Underneath that surplus, the trading arm can be quietly carried by money that was never meant to subsidise it. Overhead gets absorbed across the whole entity rather than allocated to where it belongs, and the commercial side ends up looking more efficient than it actually is.

This is not a compliance issue. It is simply what happens when restricted funding and unrestricted trading income sit in the same view without being separated.

The organisations we see handle succession and growth best are the ones whose board or council can see both ledgers clearly, the mission and the trade, reported separately rather than averaged into one number.

That separation does not change the mission. It gives leadership a genuine read on which parts of the operation pay their own way, and which depend on a funding cycle continuing on schedule, before the next grant round is the thing that forces the question.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#AustralianBusiness #FinancialStrategy #SMEFinance
facebook:
NAIDOC Week has us thinking about something a lot of Indigenous-owned enterprises and social enterprises deal with quietly all year round: grant money and trading income sitting in the same set of books.

When the two are blended together, the whole organisation can look like it is doing fine, while the actual trading side, the cafe, the consultancy, the retail arm, might be getting carried by funding that was never meant to prop it up.

Separate the two and you get a far more honest picture of what is actually paying its own way.

Does your organisation know what its trading arm would look like without the grant funding sitting underneath it?

#SocialEnterprise
google:
This is NAIDOC Week, and it has us thinking about a pattern we see across Indigenous-owned enterprises and social enterprises on the East Coast all year round, not just this week.

Many of these organisations run a genuine commercial arm, a cafe, a consultancy, a cultural tourism operation, a retail line, alongside grant funding, sponsorship and government contracts. When both sit inside the same set of accounts without being separated, the whole organisation can look financially healthy for the year while the trading side underneath is quietly being carried by funding that was never meant to subsidise it.

This is not a bookkeeping error. Grant and program income is usually restricted to a specific purpose, while trading revenue is not, and blending the two into one number makes it hard for a board or council to judge which parts of the operation are genuinely paying their own way.

Separating out the two ledgers, the funded programs and the trading arm, gives leadership a much clearer picture heading into succession and growth decisions, particularly with a new financial year now underway.

If you lead or sit on the board of an Indigenous-owned enterprise or social enterprise anywhere from Brisbane to Sydney or Melbourne, this is worth a closer look. Read the full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.
